summary: This advice column addresses three substantive questions: a woman dealing with a hypercritical boyfriend, someone asking about work-life balance and productivity, and a math professor complaining about socially awkward colleagues at conferences. Aunt Pythia advises the first writer to recognize her boyfriend's criticisms as projections of his own insecurities and reclaim her power in the relationship. For the productivity question, she shares her secrets: prioritizing sleep, saying no to most things, being ruthlessly efficient without perfectionism, and surrounding herself with trusted collaborators. The final response turns the professor's complaints back on him, suggesting his harsh judgment of colleagues as 'cripples' reveals his own social anxiety and insecurity.
key_points: Criticism often reveals the critic's own insecurities - 'people accuse you of the thing they are guilty of'
Prioritizing sleep and saying no to most commitments enables higher productivity and engagement with chosen activities
Ruthless efficiency means accepting imperfection: publish quickly, prepare talks in 1.5x their length, and move on from failures
Anxiety about social situations often manifests as harsh judgment of others
Building resilience and a strong support system allows you to take risks on trusting people
money_quotes: People accuse you of the thing they are guilty of.
I say 'no' almost all the time to almost everything, so I can spend more time doing stuff I love like watching Star Trek: Deep Space 9 with my kids and taking naps.
I generally think people shortchange their own sleep thinking it will make them more efficient, when in fact it does the opposite.
If things suck, I take it in stride, make a mental note to myself, and move on. In other words, I'm ruthlessly efficient and my skin is thick.
I give myself 1.5 times as long to prepare a talk as the talk will last, a trick I learned from my teaching experience.
There are assholes in math, but they are typically pretty minor league compared to, say, the finance assholes or the Silicon Valley assholes.
You are accusing these guys of all sorts of things that you yourself are guilty of.
key_insight: The flaws we criticize most harshly in others often mirror our own insecurities, and recognizing this projection is the first step toward both self-improvement and healthier relationships.
